judgment_en: Primordial, prosperous, beneficial, correct — the primal creative force, which is beneficial and correct in its actions.
lines_zh: 初九：潜龙勿用。
九二：见龙在田，利见大人。
九三：君子终日乾乾，夕惕若，厉，无咎。
九四：或跃在渊，无咎。
九五：飞龙在天，利见大人。
上九：亢龙有悔。 用九：见群龙无首，吉。
lines_en: First line: Hidden dragon, do not act. At the outset the potential is hidden and not yet ready to be revealed; patience and caution are advised.
Second line: The dragon appears in the field; it is beneficial to see the great man. Potential begins to manifest, and it is advantageous to seek the guidance of a wise, capable person.
Third line: The superior man is active and vigilant all day, wary at night; there is danger, but no blame. Continuous effort and vigilance allow one to avoid blame.
Fourth line: Perhaps leaping in the abyss, no blame. A moment of transition or a bold step, which if taken with the right intention will not lead to blame.
Fifth line: Flying dragon in the heavens; it is beneficial to see the great man. The potential has fully manifested at its peak of success, and it is still wise to seek counsel from capable people.

Top line: The arrogant dragon will have cause to repent — a warning against overreaching even at the height of success. (Use of Nines: seeing a group of dragons with no single leader brings good fortune, valuing collaboration and shared leadership.)

judgment_zh: 元亨，利牝馬之貞。君子有攸往，先迷後得主，利西南得朋，東北喪朋。安貞，吉。
judgment_en: Great success; the perseverance of a mare is beneficial. The superior person has a direction to go — initially lost, later finds a master. Beneficial to find friends in the southwest, losing friends in the northeast. Peaceful and steadfast brings good fortune.
lines_zh: 初六：履霜，堅冰至。
六二：直，方，大，不習无不利。
六三：含章可貞。或從王事，无成有終。
六四：括囊；无咎，无譽。
六五：黃裳，元吉。
上六：龍戰于野，其血玄黃. 用六：利永貞。
lines_en: First line: Treading on frost, solid ice will come — small signs can foretell significant outcomes.
Second line: Straight, square, and great; unpracticed yet nothing is unfavorable — natural integrity and straightforwardness lead to success.
Third line: Containing inner beauty, one can be steadfast; perhaps involved in affairs of state, with no immediate credit but a proper conclusion in the end.
Fourth line: Tying up the sack; no blame, no praise — keeping things contained and avoiding extremes.
Fifth line: A yellow skirt brings great good fortune — yellow, a central color symbolizing balance and harmony, leads to great fortune.

Top line: Dragons fight in the wilderness, their blood black and yellow — a significant conflict with profound consequences. (Use of Sixes: beneficial to be perpetually steadfast — continuous perseverance is advantageous.)

judgment_zh: 元亨，利貞，勿用有攸往，利建侯。
judgment_en: Great success; advantageous to be steadfast and correct. Do not undertake action prematurely; it is beneficial to establish leaders (feudal lords).
lines_zh: 初九：磐桓；利居貞，利建侯。
六二：屯如邅如，乘馬班如。匪寇婚媾，女子貞不字，十年乃字。
六三：即鹿无虞，惟入于林中，君子幾不如舍，往吝。
六四：乘馬班如，求婚媾，往吉，无不利。
九五：屯其膏，小貞吉，大貞凶。
上六：乘馬班如，泣血漣如。
lines_en: First line: Hesitation and lingering; advantageous to remain steadfast and to establish leadership — at the start there may be delay, so stay steadfast and build a solid foundation.

Second line: Difficulty and obstruction, riding a horse in disorderly fashion; not bandits but a marriage proposal — the woman stays chaste and does not marry until ten years pass, suggesting patience as resolution comes slowly.
Third line: Chasing a deer without a guide, entering the forest; the noble one nearly gives up, since going forward brings regret — a warning against pursuing goals without proper guidance.

Fourth line: Riding a horse in disorderly fashion, seeking a marriage alliance; going forward is auspicious with nothing unfavorable — despite initial disorder, seeking alliances brings positive outcomes.
Fifth line: Accumulating resources; modest correctness is auspicious, but excessive rigidity is inauspicious — moderation is favored over overreach.
Top line: Riding a horse in disorderly fashion, weeping tears of blood — extreme distress as difficulties reach their peak.

judgment_zh: 亨。匪我求童蒙，童蒙求我。初筮告，再三瀆，瀆則不告。利貞。
judgment_en: Success. It is not I who seek the inexperienced youth, but the youth who seeks me. The first oracle informs; asked again and again it becomes disrespectful and will not answer. Beneficial to be steadfast and correct.
lines_zh: 初六：發蒙，利用刑人，用說桎梏，以往吝。
九二：包蒙吉；納婦吉；子克家。
六三：勿用取女；見金夫，不有躬，无攸利。
六四：困蒙，吝。
六五：童蒙，吉。
上九：擊蒙；不利為寇，利禦寇。
lines_en: First line: Dispelling ignorance; beneficial to use discipline and to remove shackles, though going forward carelessly brings regret — ignorance is dispelled through discipline, but caution is still needed.

Second line: Enveloping the ignorant with tolerance brings good fortune; taking a wife brings good fortune; the son proves capable of managing the household — embracing and guiding the inexperienced leads to good outcomes.
Third line: Do not marry such a woman — one who is drawn to a man of wealth and loses herself brings no benefit, warning against attraction to status over character.
Fourth line: Trapped in ignorance brings regret and difficulty.
Fifth line: Childlike inexperience brings good fortune when properly guided.
Top line: Striking down ignorance; not beneficial to act as a robber, but beneficial to defend against robbers — one should not exploit the ignorant, but should guard against being exploited.

judgment_zh: 有孚，光亨，貞吉。利涉大川。
judgment_en: With sincerity and confidence there is light and success; perseverance brings good fortune. It is beneficial to cross the great river.
lines_zh: 初九：需于郊。利用恆，无咎。
九二：需于沙。小有言，終吉。
九三：需于泥，致寇至。
六四：需于血，出自穴。
九五：需于酒食，貞吉。
上六：入于穴，有不速之客三人來，敬之終吉。
lines_en: First line: Waiting in the outskirts; beneficial to remain constant and persistent, no blame — waiting and preparing in a safe place, with consistency preventing mistakes.
Second line: Waiting on the sand; there may be minor disputes, but in the end good fortune — small conflicts in an unstable setting resolve favorably.
Third line: Waiting in the mud, which brings the arrival of enemies — a difficult, unstable situation that can attract trouble, calling for caution.
Fourth line: Waiting in blood, emerging from the cave — a dangerous situation involving conflict or injury, yet there is a way out.
Fifth line: Waiting with wine and food; perseverance brings good fortune — a comfortable, stable stage where continued perseverance leads to good outcomes.
Top line: Entering the cave, three unexpected guests arrive; treating them with respect brings good fortune in the end.

judgment_zh: 有孚，窒。惕中吉。終凶。利見大人，不利涉大川。
judgment_en: There is sincerity but obstruction. Caution in the middle brings good fortune, but pursuing it to the end is inauspicious. Beneficial to see a great person; not beneficial to cross the great river.
lines_zh: 初六：不永所事，小有言，終吉。
九二：不克訟，歸而逋，其邑人三百戶，无眚。
六三：食舊德，貞厲，終吉，或從王事，无成。
九四：不克訟，復即命渝，安貞吉。
九五：訟元吉。
上九：或錫之鞶帶，終朝三褫之。
lines_en: First line: Do not persist in the dispute; there will be minor disputes, but in the end it turns out well.
Second line: Unable to win the lawsuit, one returns and flees; the people of the village (three hundred households) come to no harm — better to retreat than to persist in a losing conflict.
Third line: Relying on old virtues; perseverance leads to danger but ultimately good fortune; involvement in higher (royal) affairs may bring no personal achievement.
Fourth line: Unable to win the lawsuit, one returns and changes course; peace and perseverance bring good fortune.
Fifth line: The conflict, rightly handled, ends in great good fortune.
Top line: One may be awarded a belt of honor, only to have it stripped away three times before the morning ends — honors won in conflict prove unstable and fleeting.

judgment_en: Perseverance, guided by a wise and respected elder, brings good fortune without blame.
lines_zh: 初六：師出以律，否臧凶。
九二：在師中吉，无咎，王三錫命。
六三：師或輿尸，凶。
六四：師左次，无咎。
六五：田有禽，利執言，无咎。長子帥師，弟子輿尸，貞凶。
上六：大君有命，開國承家，小人勿用。
lines_en: First line: The army goes forth according to strict discipline; if not, misfortune follows.
Second line: Being at the center of the army is auspicious and without blame; the king bestows three commands, signifying high honor and responsibility.
Third line: The army may end up carrying corpses — a disastrous outcome.
Fourth line: The army camps on the left flank, a strategic move made without blame.

Fifth line: There are birds in the field, so it is beneficial to speak out, no blame; the eldest son leads the army while the younger carries the dead — perseverance in this brings misfortune if leadership falters.
Top line: The great leader has a mandate to found the state and continue the family line; petty, unworthy people should not be employed.

judgment_zh: 吉。原筮元永貞，无咎。不寧方來，後夫凶。
judgment_en: Auspicious. The original divination is fundamentally and eternally correct, without blame. When unrest arises, the latecomer will meet misfortune.
lines_zh: 初六：有孚，比之，无咎。有孚盈缶，終來有它吉。
六二：比之自內，貞吉。
六三：比之匪人。
六四：外比之，貞吉。
九五：顯比，王用三驅，失前禽。邑人不誡，吉。
上六：比之无首，凶。
lines_en: First line: There is sincerity in forming union with others, no blame; sincerity fills the vessel, ultimately bringing good fortune.
Second line: Union formed from within, perseverance brings good fortune.
Third line: Union with unworthy people brings no good — a warning against untrustworthy alliances.
Fourth line: Union with those outside one's immediate circle, perseverance brings good fortune.

Fifth line: Manifest union: the king uses a three-sided hunt and lets the game escape from the front; the townspeople are not alarmed, and it is auspicious — even an apparent loss can yield a positive result for the group.
Top line: Union without a leader ends in misfortune, warning of the need for clear direction.

title_en: The Taming Power of the Small
judgment_en: Success. Dense clouds but no rain, coming from our western outskirts — potential that has gathered but not yet been realized, requiring patience.
lines_zh: 初九：復自道，何其咎，吉。
九二：牽復，吉。
九三：輿說輻，夫妻反目。
六四：有孚，血去惕出，无咎。
九五：有孚攣如，富以其鄰。
上九：既雨既處，尚德載，婦貞厲。月幾望，君子征凶。
lines_en: First line: Returning to the path — what misfortune could there be? Good fortune. Returning to the correct way of doing things avoids misfortune.
Second line: Pulling back, good fortune. Restraining oneself and exercising self-control leads to good fortune.
Third line: The spokes of the wagon come loose, husband and wife turn against each other. Internal discord and lack of proper alignment cause relationships and endeavors to fall apart.
Fourth line: There is sincerity; blood vanishes and fear departs, no blame. Sincerity and trust resolve conflict and remove fear.
Fifth line: There is sincerity, like a binding cord; it brings wealth to its neighbor. Strong bonds of sincerity lead to mutual benefit and prosperity.

Top line: After the rain has fallen and settled, virtue is still esteemed; the woman remains chaste and firm. The moon is almost full — the noble one who goes on an expedition meets misfortune, warning that challenges remain even after success.

judgment_en: Treading on the tiger's tail — it does not bite the person, success. Even in a dangerous situation, careful and correct conduct avoids harm and brings success.
lines_zh: 初九：素履，往无咎。
九二：履道坦坦，幽人貞吉。
六三：眇能視，跛能履，履虎尾，咥人，凶。武人為于大君。
九四：履虎尾，愬愬，終吉。
九五：夬履，貞厲。
上九：視履考祥，其旋元吉。
lines_en: First line: Simple treading, proceeding brings no blame. Simplicity and straightforwardness in action avoid mistakes.
Second line: Treading a level path; the recluse's perseverance brings good fortune. Following a clear, level path lets even a reclusive person find good fortune through perseverance.

Third line: The one-eyed can see, the lame can tread — treading on the tiger's tail, it bites the person, misfortune; the warrior acts for the great lord. Limited abilities in a dangerous situation lead to harm, and caution is needed even when acting on another's behalf.
Fourth line: Treading on the tiger's tail, cautious and apprehensive — in the end, good fortune. Vigilance in a dangerous situation ultimately brings good fortune.
Fifth line: Resolute treading, perseverance brings danger. Determined action carries the risk of danger and calls for careful consideration.
Top line: Observing the treading, examining the signs — in the end, great good fortune. Careful reflection on one's actions and their consequences ensures a very good outcome.

judgment_en: Small things depart, great things arrive. Good fortune and success — a time of peace and prosperity when minor issues resolve and significant benefits come.
lines_zh: 初九：拔茅茹，以其彙，征吉。
九二：包荒，用馮河，不遐遺，朋亡，得尚于中行。
九三：无平不陂，无往不復，艱貞无咎。勿恤其孚，于食有福。
六四：翩翩，不富，以其鄰，不戒以孚。
六五：帝乙歸妹，以祉元吉。
上六：城復于隍，勿用師。自邑告命，貞吝。
lines_en: First line: Pulling up thatch grass, tied together with its kind — going forward brings good fortune. Unity and collective effort lead to success.

Second line: Embracing the wilderness, crossing the river without leaving anyone behind; friends are lost, yet one gains honor in the middle path. Taking on challenges while keeping integrity and balance brings honor even amid loss.

Third line: No level ground without a slope, no going without returning; perseverance in difficulty brings no blame. Do not worry over trust — there is blessing in food. Life is cyclical, and maintaining faith through hardship brings blessings.
Fourth line: Light and graceful, not wealthy, but with neighbors; no need for warnings, trust is present. A modest life built on community and mutual trust rather than wealth.
Fifth line: Emperor Yi marries off his sister, bringing great blessings and supreme good fortune. A symbolic auspicious union brings prosperity and harmony.

Top line: The city wall returns to the moat — do not use the army; announce the mandate from the town, perseverance brings regret. A warning against military force; peaceful, internal action is better, though caution is still needed.

judgment_zh: 否之匪人，不利君子貞，大往小來。
judgment_en: Stagnation for the unworthy; it is not beneficial for the noble person to remain steadfast — the great departs, the small arrives.
lines_zh: 初六：拔茅茹，以其彙，貞吉亨。
六二：包承。小人吉，大人否，亨。
六三：包羞。
九四：有命，无咎，疇離祉。
九五：休否，大人吉。其亡其亡，繫于苞桑。
上九：傾否，先否後喜。
lines_en: First line: Pulling up thatch grass, bound together with its kind; steadfastness brings good fortune and success.
Second line: Enveloping and bearing. For the small person it is auspicious; for the great person it is stagnation, though success is still possible.
Third line: Enveloping shame.
Fourth line: There is a mandate, no blame; companions are separated from blessings.
Fifth line: Ending of stagnation, the great person is auspicious. It is as if lost, but bound fast to mulberry trees, suggesting security amid apparent peril.
Top line: Overturning stagnation — first there is stagnation, then there is joy.

judgment_zh: 同人于野，亨。利涉大川，利君子貞。
judgment_en: Fellowship with men in the open — success. It is favorable to cross the great river; the perseverance of a nobleman brings advantage. Success comes from unity and cooperation.
lines_zh: 初九：同人于門，无咎。
六二：同人于宗，吝。
九三：伏戎于莽，升其高陵，三歲不興。
九四：乘其墉，弗克攻，吉。
九五：同人，先號咷而後笑。大師克相遇。
上九：同人于郊，无悔。
lines_en: First line: Fellowship with men at the gate, no blame. Initial efforts to form alliances are a good, faultless start.
Second line: Fellowship with men in the clan, regret. Forming alliances only within one's own circle may lead to regret; one should look beyond it for true fellowship.
Third line: Hiding weapons in the thicket, ascending the high hill — for three years, one does not rise. Warns against hidden dangers and avoiding confrontation, which leads to stagnation.
Fourth line: Riding on the wall, unable to attack — good fortune. A defensive, restrained stance rather than aggression brings good fortune.
Fifth line: Fellowship with men, first weeping and lamenting, then laughing; the great leader meets with success. Initial hardship gives way to eventual triumph through perseverance.
Top line: Fellowship with men in the outskirts, no regret. Seeking unity even in distant or unfamiliar places is without regret.

judgment_en: Supreme success — a time of great power and abundance that can be used to achieve significant, beneficial outcomes.
lines_zh: 初九：无交害，匪咎，艱則无咎。
九二：大車以載，有攸往，无咎。
九三：公用亨于天子，小人弗克。
九四：匪其彭，无咎。
六五：厥孚交如，威如；吉。
上九：自天祐之，吉无不利。
lines_en: First line: No interaction with harm, no fault; if there is difficulty, there will be no fault. Avoiding harmful interactions and remaining upright prevents fault even amid difficulty.
Second line: A large cart to carry, there is somewhere to go, no fault. Having the capacity and resources to undertake significant endeavors and moving forward with purpose brings no fault.
Third line: The noble one is successful with the ruler; the petty person cannot achieve this. A person of noble character finds favor with those in power, unlike one of lesser character.
Fourth line: Not his own strength, no fault. One should not rely solely on personal power; recognizing its limits and seeking support avoids fault.
Fifth line: His trust is mutual, his majesty is awe-inspiring; good fortune. Mutual trust combined with a dignified presence leads to good fortune.
Top line: Blessed by heaven, good fortune, nothing that is not beneficial. Divine favor brings overall good fortune and universal benefit.

judgment_en: Success. The superior man has a good end — humility leads to success and a favorable outcome.
lines_zh: 初六：謙謙君子，用涉大川，吉。
六二：鳴謙，貞吉。
九三：勞謙，君子有終，吉。
六四：无不利，撝謙。
六五：不富，以其鄰，利用侵伐，无不利。
上六：鳴謙，利用行師，征邑國。
lines_en: First line: Modest, modest, the superior man — it is favorable to cross the great river, good fortune. A truly modest person can undertake great endeavors and find good fortune.
Second line: Proclaiming modesty, perseverance brings good fortune. Openly displaying modesty while maintaining integrity leads to good outcomes.
Third line: Working with modesty; the superior man has a good end, good fortune. Hard work combined with humility ensures a good outcome.
Fourth line: There is no disadvantage; maintain modesty. There are no downsides to continuing to uphold humility.
Fifth line: Not rich, but with neighbors; it is favorable to undertake punitive expeditions, no disadvantage. Even without wealth, good relations with neighbors and just action bring no harm.
Top line: Proclaiming modesty; it is favorable to lead an army and conquer the city-state. Openly displayed modesty can be advantageous even in leadership and conquest.

judgment_en: It is beneficial to establish feudal lords and to mobilize the army — enthusiasm and joy can lead to successful leadership and effective mobilization.
lines_zh: 初六：鳴豫，凶。
六二：介于石，不終日，貞吉。
六三：盱豫，悔。遲有悔。
九四：由豫，大有得。勿疑。朋盍簪。
六五：貞疾，恆不死。
上六：冥豫，成有渝，无咎。
lines_en: First line: Enthusiastic singing, misfortune. Excessive enthusiasm or premature celebration at the outset leads to misfortune; caution against overconfidence.
Second line: Firm as a rock, not lasting the whole day, perseverance brings good fortune. Steadfastness and integrity through brief challenges bring good fortune.
Third line: Hesitant enthusiasm, regret; delay brings regret. Hesitation or indecision in the face of enthusiasm leads to regret, worsened by delay.
Fourth line: Following enthusiasm, great gains; do not doubt, friends gather like hairpins. Pursuing enthusiasm without doubt leads to significant gains and attracts allies.
Fifth line: Perseverance in illness, constant and not dying. Even amid adversity, perseverance and constancy ensure survival.
Top line: Darkened enthusiasm, success brings change, no blame. At the highest point enthusiasm may become obscured, but the resulting change brings success without blame.

judgment_en: Great success, beneficial to be steadfast and upright, no blame.
lines_zh: 初九：官有渝，貞吉。出門交有功。
六二：系小子，失丈夫。
六三：系丈夫，失小子。隨有求得，利居貞。
九四：隨有獲，貞凶。有孚在道，以明，何咎。
九五：孚于嘉，吉。
上六：拘系之，乃從維之。王用亨于西山。
lines_en: First line: There is a change in official position; being steadfast and upright brings good fortune. Going out and interacting with others brings success — changes in one's role can be beneficial if one remains upright, and engaging with others outside one's usual environment leads to success.
Second line: Tying up a young boy, losing a mature man — focusing on trivial matters can lead to the loss of more important things.

Third line: Tying up a mature man, losing a young boy; following and seeking will bring results, and it is beneficial to remain steadfast and upright — focusing on important matters may mean neglecting trivial ones, but diligent pursuit brings results.

Fourth line: Following brings gains, but steadfastness leads to misfortune; yet if there is sincerity in the path, with clarity, what blame could there be? Gains from following can turn to misfortune if one is too rigid, but sincerity and clarity avoid blame.
Fifth line: Sincerity towards what is excellent brings good fortune.

Top line: Binding and restraining, then following and supporting; the king offers sacrifices on the western mountain — initial restraint gives way to support, symbolizing a successful and auspicious outcome.

title_en: Decay (Work on What Has Been Spoiled)
judgment_zh: 元亨，利涉大川。先甲三日，後甲三日。
judgment_en: Great success. It is beneficial to cross the great river. Before the first day of the cycle, three days; after the first day of the cycle, three days — despite the decay, there is potential for great success in taking on significant challenges, with careful planning and timing being crucial.
lines_zh: 初六：幹父之蠱，有子，考无咎，厲終吉。
九二：幹母之蠱，不可貞。
九三：幹父之蠱，小有悔，无大咎。
六四：裕父之蠱，往見吝。
六五：幹父之蠱，用譽。
上九：不事王侯，高尚其事。
lines_en: First line: Working on the decay caused by the father. If there is a son (successor), there will be no blame; danger will end in good fortune — inherited problems can be resolved without blame, and though there may be danger, it ultimately leads to a positive outcome.
Second line: Working on the decay caused by the mother. It is not advisable to be steadfast — being too rigid in addressing issues inherited from the mother is not advisable.
Third line: Working on the decay caused by the father. There will be minor regrets, but no major blame.
Fourth line: Indulging the decay caused by the father. Going forward will lead to distress — failing to address inherited issues and instead indulging them leads to regret.
Fifth line: Working on the decay caused by the father. This will bring praise — resolving inherited issues leads to recognition.
Top line: Not serving kings and princes; he maintains his high principles — prioritizing personal integrity over serving those in power.

judgment_zh: 元，亨，利，貞。至于八月有凶。
judgment_en: Great success, smooth progress, benefit, and correctness. However, by the eighth month, there will be misfortune — a time of great potential and success, but this favorable period may not last indefinitely.
lines_zh: 初九：咸臨，貞吉。
九二：咸臨，吉无不利。
六三：甘臨，无攸利。既憂之，无咎。
六四：至臨，无咎。
六五：知臨，大君之宜，吉。
上六：敦臨，吉无咎。
lines_en: First line: Sincere approach, correct and auspicious — a sincere and correct approach leads to good fortune.
Second line: Sincere approach, auspicious and without any disadvantage.
Third line: Sweet (overly pleasant) approach, no benefit; but if there is worry/caution, there will be no blame.
Fourth line: Arriving approach, no blame — approaching or coming closer in a proper manner results in no blame.
Fifth line: Wise approach, suitable for a great leader, auspicious.
Top line: Respectful approach, auspicious and without blame.

judgment_en: Washing hands but not offering a sacrifice, there is sincerity and reverence — a state of purity and sincerity without the need for formal offerings, emphasizing inner sincerity over outward ritual.
lines_zh: 初六：童觀，小人无咎，君子吝。
六二：闚觀，利女貞。
六三：觀我生，進退。
六四：觀國之光，利用賓于王。
九五：觀我生，君子无咎。
上九：觀其生，君子无咎。
lines_en: First line: Childlike contemplation; for the small (ordinary) person there is no blame, but for the superior person there is regret — a naive perspective is harmless for ordinary people but unsuitable for one of higher moral standing, who should seek deeper understanding.
Second line: Peeping (narrow) contemplation; it is beneficial for a woman to be chaste — a superficial or limited view, where maintaining integrity is beneficial.
Third line: Contemplating my life, advancing and retreating — self-examination and the need to carefully consider one's actions.

Fourth line: Contemplating the light (splendor) of the country; it is beneficial to be a guest of the king — observing the nation's splendor implies advantage in being in the presence of a wise ruler.
Fifth line: Contemplating my life; the superior person has no blame — self-reflection leads a person of high moral standing to find no fault in themselves.
Top line: Contemplating their (others') life; the superior person has no blame — observing the lives of others also leads to a fault-free state, emphasizing understanding and empathy.

judgment_en: Success. It is beneficial to use legal proceedings — success can be achieved by addressing problems directly, possibly through legal or formal means.
lines_zh: 初九：屨校滅趾，无咎。
六二：噬膚滅鼻，无咎。
六三：噬臘肉，遇毒；小吝，无咎。
九四：噬乾胏，得金矢，利艱貞，吉。
六五：噬乾肉，得黃金，貞厲，无咎。
上九：何校滅耳，凶。
lines_en: First line: Wearing a wooden clog, one loses a toe. No blame — a minor injury or loss due to an obstruction, but not a serious fault.
Second line: Biting through the skin, one loses the nose. No blame — a more significant injury while resolving an issue, but no blame because the action was necessary.
Third line: Biting into dried meat, one encounters poison; slight humiliation, no blame — unexpected difficulty in a tough situation brings minor setback or embarrassment but ultimately no blame.
Fourth line: Biting into dried bones, one finds a golden arrow; it is beneficial to be firm and correct, good fortune — perseverance through difficulty can lead to valuable rewards.
Fifth line: Biting into dried meat, one finds yellow gold; being correct is dangerous, but there is no blame — tough situations can yield valuable results, though maintaining correctness is risky.
Top line: Wearing a wooden clog, one loses an ear. Misfortune — severe consequences from obstruction result in misfortune.

judgment_en: Success. It is beneficial to have somewhere to go — signifies beauty, grace, and adornment; while there is success, the benefits are small, and it is advantageous to have a clear direction.
lines_zh: 初九：賁其趾，舍車而徒。
六二：賁其須。
九三：賁如濡如，永貞吉。
六四：賁如皤如，白馬翰如，匪寇婚媾。
六五：賁于丘園，束帛戔戔，吝，終吉。
上九：白賁，无咎。
lines_en: First line: Adorning the toes, abandoning the carriage and walking — starting with small, simple steps, implying a need for humility and simplicity.
Second line: Adorning the beard — a focus on superficial appearances, cautioning against overemphasizing external beauty.
Third line: Adorned as if moist, constant correctness brings good fortune — being well-prepared and adaptable, with integrity bringing lasting good fortune.
Fourth line: Adorned as if white, a white horse with wings, not a bandit but a suitor — a situation that appears threatening is actually benign or beneficial; appearances can be deceiving.
Fifth line: Adorned in the hills and gardens, a small bundle of silk; regret, but in the end good fortune — modesty and simplicity with small offerings bring initial regret but eventual good fortune.
Top line: Pure adornment, no blame — a state of pure and simple beauty, with no fault.

judgment_en: It is not beneficial to have somewhere to go — a situation of disintegration or decay; it advises against taking action or making progress, as circumstances are unfavorable.
lines_zh: 初六：剝床以足，蔑貞凶。
六二：剝床以辨，蔑貞凶。
六三：剝之，无咎。
六四：剝床以膚，凶。
六五：貫魚，以宮人寵，无不利。
上九：碩果不食，君子得輿，小人剝廬。
lines_en: First line: Splitting the bed at the legs, no constancy, misfortune — the foundation is being damaged, indicating instability and lack of support.
Second line: Splitting the bed at the frame, no constancy, misfortune — damage has moved up to the frame, further indicating instability.
Third line: Splitting it, no blame — the splitting is acknowledged, but recognizing the problem is a step toward resolving it.
Fourth line: Splitting the bed at the skin, misfortune — the damage has reached the surface, now visible and severe.
Fifth line: A string of fish, with the favor of the palace maid, nothing is unfavorable — despite disintegration, things are held together and there is support or benefit.
Top line: The large fruit is not eaten; the noble person gets a carriage, the small person loses their house — a divergence in outcomes based on one's character and actions.

judgment_zh: 亨。出入无疾，朋來无咎。反復其道，七日來復，利有攸往。
judgment_en: Success. Going out and coming in without illness, friends come without blame. Returning repeatedly to the path, in seven days comes the return; it is beneficial to have somewhere to go — symbolizes returning to the correct path, a period of renewal and recovery, successful and free from harm.
lines_zh: 初九：不遠復，无祗悔，元吉。
六二：休復，吉。
六三：頻復，厲无咎。
六四：中行獨復。
六五：敦復，无悔。
上六：迷復，凶，有災眚。用行師，終有大敗，以其國君，凶；至于十年，不克征。
lines_en: First line: Not far to return, no need for remorse, great fortune — the return is not far off and there is no need for regret, indicating a positive outcome.
Second line: Restful return, good fortune — a peaceful and restful return brings good fortune.
Third line: Frequent return, danger but no blame — frequent returns may bring danger, but ultimately there will be no blame.
Fourth line: Walking in the center, returning alone — a solitary return while maintaining a balanced path.
Fifth line: Sincere return, no remorse — a sincere and earnest return, free from remorse.

Top line: Lost return, misfortune, there is disaster and calamity. Using the army, there will be a great defeat, the ruler of the country will face misfortune; for ten years, it will not be possible to make an expedition — a misguided return leading to disaster; it advises against military action, predicting significant defeat and prolonged difficulties.

judgment_zh: 元亨，利貞。其匪正有眚，不利有攸往。
judgment_en: Great success. Advantageous to be steadfast and upright. If actions are not correct, misfortune will follow, and it is not advantageous to proceed with plans.
lines_zh: 初九：无妄，往吉。
六二：不耕獲，不菑畬，則利有攸往。
六三：无妄之災，或繫之牛，行人之得，邑人之災。
九四：可貞，无咎。
九五：无妄之疾，勿藥有喜。
上九：无妄，行有眚，无攸利。
lines_en: First line: Innocence; going forward is auspicious. Maintaining innocence and sincerity in one's actions leads to good fortune.
Second line: Not plowing yet reaping, not clearing yet cultivating; it is then advantageous to have somewhere to go. Results can come even without visible effort, so it is favorable to proceed.

Third line: The disaster of innocence: someone ties up an ox; the traveler's gain becomes the townspeople's loss. An unexpected mishap benefits one party while harming another, showing that innocence can still lead to unforeseen consequences.
Fourth line: Can remain steadfast; no blame. Being upright and steady leads to no fault.
Fifth line: The illness of innocence; do not medicate, and there will be joy. A problem arising without real cause should be left untreated, as it will resolve naturally into a good outcome.
Top line: Innocence; yet acting now brings misfortune, no advantage. Even in innocence, pressing forward at this point leads to misfortune, with nothing to be gained.

judgment_zh: 利貞，不家食吉，利涉大川。
judgment_en: Favorable to be steadfast and upright. Not eating at home is auspicious, showing self-restraint and discipline. Favorable to cross the great river, i.e. to undertake significant ventures.
lines_zh: 初九：有厲利已。
九二：輿說輹。
九三：良馬逐，利艱貞。曰閑輿衛，利有攸往。
六四：童牛之牿，元吉。
六五：豶豕之牙，吉。
上九：何天之衢，亨。
lines_en: First line: There is danger, but it is beneficial to stop. Recognizing potential danger and halting in time proves beneficial.
Second line: The carriage loses its axle. Progress is hindered, symbolizing a need for repair or re-evaluation before moving on.

Third line: Good horses give chase; favorable to remain steadfast amid difficulty. It is said one guards the carriage; favorable to have a destination. Effort and pursuit paired with readiness and a clear goal bring benefit.
Fourth line: The young ox's horns are bound; great good fortune. Control and restraint applied early lead to great fortune.
Fifth line: The tusks of a castrated boar; auspicious. A lack of aggression makes the situation auspicious.
Top line: What a heavenly path; success. Indicates a higher level of success and fulfillment.

judgment_en: Perseverance brings good fortune. Observe how nourishment works, and seek your own proper sustenance.
lines_zh: 初九：舍爾靈龜，觀我朵頤，凶。
六二：顛頤，拂經，于丘頤，征凶。
六三：拂頤，貞凶，十年勿用，无攸利。
六四：顛頤，吉，虎視眈眈，其欲逐逐，无咎。
六五：拂經，居貞吉，不可涉大川。
上九：由頤，厲吉，利涉大川。
lines_en: First line: Abandon your sacred tortoise, and watch my jaw move enviously; inauspicious. Warns against abandoning one's own wisdom or guidance to enviously follow others, which leads to misfortune.
Second line: Upside-down nourishment, deviating from the proper path, seeking nourishment on a hill; leads to misfortune. Improper or misguided efforts to seek nourishment bring negative outcomes.

Third line: Deviating from nourishment; persisting in this brings misfortune; do not act on this for ten years, there is no benefit. Persisting in harmful ways of seeking sustenance calls for a long period of caution and inaction.

Fourth line: Upside-down nourishment, yet good fortune; the tiger watches intently, its desire pursuing relentlessly; no blame. Even an unconventional approach to nourishment can succeed if pursued with vigilance and focus, like a tiger.

Fifth line: Deviating from the path, but remaining steadfast brings good fortune; do not cross the great river. Deviation from the norm can still bring good fortune if one stays steadfast, but one should avoid overly risky ventures.

Top line: Following the source of nourishment; there is danger but good fortune; favorable to cross the great river. Following the correct path of nourishment despite danger leads to good fortune, favorable even for great undertakings.

judgment_en: The ridgepole sags; it is beneficial to have somewhere to go; success. Represents a state of excess or imbalance and structural weakness, yet having direction and purpose brings success.
lines_zh: 初六：藉用白茅，无咎。
九二：枯楊生稊，老夫得其女妻，无不利。
九三：棟橈，凶。
九四：棟隆，吉；有它吝。
九五：枯楊生華，老婦得士夫，无咎无譽。
上六：過涉滅頂，凶，无咎。
lines_en: First line: Use white rushes as a mat beneath the offering; no blame. A humble, simple material signifies modesty and care, which avoids fault.
Second line: A withered poplar sprouts new shoots; an old man takes a young wife; nothing is unfavorable. Symbolizes renewal and unexpected benefit, a rejuvenation or new beginning.
Third line: The ridgepole sags; misfortune. Indicates structural weakness leading to potential disaster.
Fourth line: The ridgepole is raised up; good fortune; but there is something else to be cautious about. Strengthening the structure brings good fortune, though hidden concerns remain.
Fifth line: A withered poplar produces flowers; an old woman takes a young husband; no blame, no praise. A surprising change that is neutral — neither praiseworthy nor blameworthy.
Top line: Wading across until the water submerges the head; misfortune, yet no blame. Being overwhelmed leads to misfortune, but it is not one's own fault.

judgment_zh: 習坎，有孚，維心亨，行有尚。
judgment_en: Repeated peril; there is sincerity, the heart finds success, and actions are esteemed. Represents danger and difficulty, through which sincerity and a steady heart bring one safely through.
lines_zh: 初六：習坎，入于坎窞，凶。
九二：坎有險，求小得。
六三：來之坎坎，險且枕，入于坎窞，勿用。
六四：樽酒簋貳，用缶，納約自牖，終无咎。
九五：坎不盈，祗既平，无咎。
上六：係用徽纆，寘于叢棘，三歲不得，凶。
lines_en: First line: Repeated peril, falling into the pit's depths; ominous. Falling repeatedly into the same difficulty without change leads to misfortune.
Second line: There is danger in the pit; seeking only small gains. Despite the danger, small successes are possible through caution.

Third line: Coming again and again to peril, dangerous footing, sinking into the pit's depths; do not act. Warns against complacency amid repeated danger — the situation worsens, and further action should be avoided.
Fourth line: A jar of wine and a pair of bowls, used simply, offered humbly through the window; no blame in the end. Simplicity and humility in accepting modest means avoid fault.
Fifth line: The pit is not overflowing, only level; no blame. The danger has been managed to a stable, controlled state.
Top line: Bound with cords and ropes, placed among thorn bushes; for three years finding no way out; ominous. Prolonged entrapment without resolution leads to misfortune.

judgment_en: Advantageous to be steadfast and upright; success. Rearing a cow brings good fortune. Symbolizes fire and light — adherence to principle brings success, and nurturing care leads to good outcomes.
lines_zh: 初九：履錯然，敬之无咎。
六二：黃離，元吉。
九三：日昃之離，不鼓缶而歌，則大耋之嗟，凶。
九四：突如其來如，焚如，死如，棄如。
六五：出涕沱若，戚嗟若，吉。
上九：王用出征，有嘉折首，獲匪其醜，无咎。
lines_en: First line: Treading with confusion, yet acting with respect brings no blame. Even amid uncertainty, maintaining respect and caution prevents misfortune.
Second line: Yellow clinging; great good fortune. Yellow represents the center and balance; holding to a balanced, central path brings great fortune.

Third line: The clinging light of the setting sun; not drumming and singing brings the lament of old age; misfortune. Suggests decline — failing to act in time leads to regret and misfortune in later years.
Fourth line: It comes on suddenly — flaring up, dying out, cast aside. Describes a sudden, intense event leading to destruction and abandonment.
Fifth line: Tears flowing like a flood, sighing and lamenting; good fortune. Intense emotional release; despite the sorrow, it leads to a good outcome.

Top line: The king uses this to go on campaign; there is great achievement, capturing the leaders but not the rank and file; no blame. A leader undertakes a mission and achieves a favorable outcome without fault.

judgment_en: Success; beneficial to be steadfast and upright; taking a wife is auspicious. Signifies mutual influence and attraction, especially in relationships — forming a union is favorable.
lines_zh: 初六，咸其拇。
六二，咸其腓，凶，居吉。
九三，咸其股，執其隨，往吝。
九四，貞吉，悔亡，憧憧往來，朋從爾思。
九五，咸其脢，无悔。
上六，咸其輔頰舌。
lines_en: First line: Influence felt in the big toe. The earliest stage of influence, just beginning to stir and not yet fully developed.
Second line: Influence felt in the calves; misfortune, but staying put is auspicious. A stronger yet still unstable influence — remaining still rather than acting avoids misfortune.
Third line: Influence felt in the thighs; clinging to what follows brings regret. A more significant influence; holding too tightly to impulse leads to regret, calling for balance and caution.
Fourth line: Steadfastness brings good fortune, regret vanishes; restless coming and going, friends follow your thoughts. A clear, thoughtful approach attracts like-minded companions.
Fifth line: Influence felt in the back of the heart; no regret. A deep, settled influence bringing stability and support, with no cause for regret.
Top line: Influence felt in the jaws, cheeks, and tongue. The influence reaches its fullest, most expressive stage, affecting speech and communication with others.

judgment_en: Success, no blame, favorable to be steadfast and upright, favorable to have somewhere to go.
lines_zh: 初六：浚恆，貞凶，无攸利。
九二：悔亡。
九三：不恆其德，或承之羞，貞吝。
九四：田无禽。
六五：恆其德，貞，婦人吉，夫子凶。
上六：振恆，凶。
lines_en: First line: Deep, excessive perseverance; even uprightness leads to misfortune, no benefit in having a direction to pursue. Rigid perseverance without flexibility can turn to misfortune.
Second line: Regret vanishes. Previous mistakes or regrets fade away, a favorable sign so long as one maintains steady perseverance.
Third line: Failing to persevere in one's virtue may invite shame; persisting in this brings difficulty. Neglecting moral integrity leads to shame and hardship.
Fourth line: In the field, no game is caught. Suggests a lack of results — effort spent may prove fruitless in this situation.
Fifth line: Perseverance in virtue, remaining upright; favorable for a woman, unfavorable for a man. Constancy in virtue is judged differently depending on one's role.
Top line: Shaking, unstable perseverance; misfortune. Inconsistency or instability in one's constancy leads to misfortune.

judgment_en: Success. Small gains are beneficial if one remains correct.
lines_zh: 初六：遯尾，厲，勿用有攸往。
六二：執之用黃牛之革，莫之勝說。
九三：係遯，有疾厲，畜臣妾吉。
九四：好遯，君子吉，小人否。
九五：嘉遯，貞吉。
上九：肥遯，无不利。
lines_en: First line: Retreating by the tail — a hesitant, last-minute retreat; there is danger. Do not act or go anywhere in this situation.
Second line: Binding it with the hide of a yellow ox, so firmly that no one can untie it — the matter is securely and reliably held in check.
Third line: A tied, constrained retreat brings illness and danger; but caring for one's servants and dependents is auspicious.
Fourth line: A good, well-executed retreat: the noble person finds good fortune, but the petty person does not.
Fifth line: An excellent, highly favorable retreat; remaining correct brings good fortune.
Top line: An abundant, well-resourced retreat; nothing is unfavorable.

judgment_en: It is beneficial to be steadfast and correct.
lines_zh: 初九：壯于趾，征凶，有孚。
九二：貞吉。
九三：小人用壯，君子用罔，貞厲。羝羊觸藩，羸其角。
九四：貞吉悔亡，藩決不羸，壯于大輿之輹。
六五：喪羊于易，无悔。
上六：羝羊觸藩，不能退，不能遂，无攸利，艱則吉。
lines_en: First line: Strength shows in the toes; advancing now leads to misfortune, though sincerity is present.
Second line: Steadfastness brings good fortune.
Third line: The inferior man relies on brute strength while the superior man uses restraint; persisting rigidly leads to danger, like a ram that butts a fence and gets its horns entangled.
Fourth line: Steadfastness brings good fortune and regret vanishes; the fence gives way and the ram is no longer entangled — strength is now well-placed, like the axle of a great wagon.
Fifth line: Losing the ram in easy circumstances brings no regret.
Top line: A ram butts against a fence, unable to retreat or advance, with no benefit in any direction; yet enduring the hardship will eventually bring good fortune.

judgment_zh: 康侯用錫馬蕃庶，晝日三接。
judgment_en: The prince of Kang bestows horses upon the multitude and meets with them three times in a day.
lines_zh: 初六：晉如，摧如，貞吉。罔孚，裕无咎。
六二：晉如，愁如，貞吉。受茲介福，于其王母。
六三：眾允，悔亡。
九四：晉如碩鼠，貞厲。
六五：悔亡，失得勿恤，往吉，无不利。
上九：晉其角，維用伐邑，厲吉无咎，貞吝。
lines_en: First line: Progressing yet encountering obstacles; perseverance brings good fortune. Though trust is lacking, generosity brings no blame.
Second line: Progressing with anxiety; perseverance brings good fortune, and one receives great blessings from the queen mother.
Third line: The multitude is in agreement; regret vanishes.
Fourth line: Progressing like a large rat (in a sneaky, underhanded manner); persisting this way leads to danger.
Fifth line: Regret vanishes; do not worry about loss or gain — moving forward brings good fortune, and nothing is unfavorable.
Top line: Progressing with horns, used to attack a city; danger brings good fortune without blame, though persisting rigidly leads to distress.

judgment_en: A time when clarity and brightness are obscured; it is beneficial to persevere firmly and correctly through hardship.
lines_zh: 初九：明夷于飛，垂其翼。君子于行，三日不食，有攸往，主人有言。
六二：明夷，夷于左股，用拯馬壯，吉。
九三：明夷于南狩，得其大首，不可疾貞。
六四：入于左腹，獲明夷之心，出于門庭。
六五：箕子之明夷，利貞。
上六：不明晦，初登于天，後入于地。
lines_en: First line: Darkening of the light while flying, he lowers his wings; the superior man on his journey does not eat for three days. He has somewhere to go, and the host has something to say — a time calling for cautious, patient retreat even amid hardship.
Second line: Darkening of the light: he is injured in the left thigh, but a strong horse comes to his rescue. Good fortune.
Third line: Darkening of the light while hunting in the south, he captures the great leader; it is not advisable to be too hasty or rigid.
Fourth line: Entering the left side of the belly, he grasps the heart of the darkening light, and emerges successfully into the courtyard.
Fifth line: The darkening of the light of Prince Ji; it is beneficial to remain firm and correct.
Top line: Not bright but dark: first ascending to heaven, then descending into the earth — the cyclical turn from success to obscurity.

judgment_en: It is beneficial for the woman to be chaste and correct.
lines_zh: 初九：閑有家，悔亡。
六二：无攸遂，在中饋，貞吉。
九三：家人嗃嗃，悔厲吉；婦子嘻嘻，終吝。
六四：富家，大吉。
九五：王假有家，勿恤，往吉。
上九：有孚威如，終吉。
lines_en: First line: When the family is kept in order, regret vanishes.
Second line: There is no place to pursue outward achievement; remaining in the central role of managing the household, correctness brings good fortune.
Third line: The family members are stern and strict, which brings regret and danger but ultimately good fortune; if the wife and children are frivolous, it ends in disgrace.
Fourth line: The family is wealthy; great good fortune.
Fifth line: The king approaches the family; there is no need for worry, and it is auspicious to proceed.
Top line: There is sincerity and an awe-inspiring presence; ultimately good fortune.

judgment_en: Small matters are auspicious.
lines_zh: 初九：悔亡，喪馬勿逐，自復；見惡人无咎。
九二：遇主于巷，无咎。
六三：見輿曳，其牛掣，其人天且劓，无初有終。
九四：睽孤，遇元夫，交孚，厲无咎。
六五：悔亡，厥宗噬膚，往何咎。
上九：睽孤，見豕負塗，載鬼一車，先張之弧，後說之弧，匪寇婚媾，往遇雨則吉。
lines_en: First line: Regret vanishes; if you lose a horse, do not chase it — it will return on its own. Encountering a bad person brings no blame.
Second line: Meeting one's master in the alley; no blame.
Third line: Seeing a cart being dragged back and its ox held back, its occupant branded and mutilated; there is no good beginning, but there will be a resolution in the end.
Fourth line: Isolated in opposition, he meets a great man; through mutual trust, though there is danger, there is no blame.
Fifth line: Regret vanishes; his kinsman bites through the skin, overcoming superficial issues — going forward, what blame could there be?

Top line: Isolated in opposition, seeing a mud-covered pig and a cart full of ghosts; first drawing the bow as if for battle, then relaxing it — they are not bandits but a marriage party. Going forward and meeting rain is auspicious.

judgment_zh: 利西南，不利東北；利見大人，貞吉。
judgment_en: It is advantageous to go southwest, but not beneficial to go northeast. It is beneficial to meet a great person; correctness brings good fortune.
lines_zh: 初六：往蹇，來譽。
六二：王臣蹇蹇，匪躬之故。
九三：往蹇來反。
六四：往蹇來連。
九五：大蹇朋來。
上六：往蹇來碩，吉；利見大人。
lines_en: First line: Going forward meets obstruction, but coming back (retreating or reconsidering) brings praise.
Second line: The king's minister faces great difficulty, not because of any fault of his own — the challenge is external.
Third line: Going forward meets obstruction; coming back leads to reversal, a change of course.
Fourth line: Going forward meets obstruction; coming back leads to new connections or opportunities.
Fifth line: Great obstruction; but friends and allies come to help.
Top line: Going forward meets obstruction; coming back leads to great achievement — auspicious. It is beneficial to meet a great person.

judgment_zh: 利西南，无所往，其來復吉。有攸往，夙吉。
judgment_en: Favorable in the southwest. If there is nowhere to go, returning is auspicious; if there is somewhere to go, acting early brings good fortune.
lines_zh: 初六：无咎。
九二：田獲三狐，得黃矢，貞吉。
六三：負且乘，致寇至，貞吝。
九四：解而拇，朋至斯孚。
六五：君子維有解，吉；有孚于小人。
上六：公用射隼于高墉之上，獲之，无不利。
lines_en: First line: No blame.
Second line: In the field, three foxes are caught and yellow arrows obtained; perseverance brings good fortune.
Third line: Carrying a burden while riding invites bandits to arrive; persisting in this brings distress.
Fourth line: Untangling the big toe — resolving a minor issue; friends arrive and trust is established.
Fifth line: The noble person achieves release — auspicious; trust extends even to lesser people.
Top line: The duke shoots a hawk perched atop a high wall and captures it; nothing is unfavorable.

judgment_zh: 有孚，元吉，无咎，可貞，利有攸往。曷之用，二簋可用享。
judgment_en: With sincerity, great fortune, no blame; it is permissible to be steadfast. It is beneficial to have somewhere to go. What is it used for? Two bowls can be used for offerings.
lines_zh: 初九：巳事遄往，无咎，酌損之。
九二：利貞，征凶，弗損，益之。
六三：三人行，則損一人；一人行，則得其友。
六四：損其疾，使遄有喜，无咎。
六五：或益之，十朋之龜弗克違，元吉。
上九：弗損益之，无咎，貞吉，有攸往，得臣无家。
lines_en: First line: If the task is completed quickly, there will be no blame; consider carefully what should be decreased. This advises prompt action to avoid blame while thoughtfully reducing what needs to be reduced.
Second line: It is beneficial to be steadfast; going on an expedition brings misfortune. If you do not decrease, you will increase — emphasizing steadfastness over rash new ventures.

Third line: When three people walk together, one will be lost; when one person walks alone, he will find his friend. A group may lose a member, but a solitary traveler is more likely to find a true companion.
Fourth line: Decrease your faults, and you will quickly find joy; there will be no blame. Reducing one's negative traits brings swift happiness and avoids fault.
Fifth line: Someone may increase it; the tortoise shell of ten friends cannot be opposed. Great fortune. The collective wisdom and support of friends is unassailable, bringing great fortune.
Top line: If you do not decrease but increase, there will be no blame; steadfast and fortunate, there is somewhere to go, and you will gain followers without a fixed home.

judgment_en: It is beneficial to have somewhere to go; it is beneficial to cross the great river — taking action and moving forward will bring benefits.
lines_zh: 初九：利用為大作，元吉，无咎。
六二：或益之，十朋之龜弗克違，永貞吉。王用享于帝，吉。
六三：益之用凶事，无咎。有孚中行，告公用圭。
六四：中行，告公從。利用為依遷國。
九五：有孚惠心，勿問元吉。有孚惠我德。
上九：莫益之，或擊之，立心勿恆，凶。
lines_en: First line: It is beneficial to undertake great works; great fortune, no blame. Initiating significant projects leads to great success without negative consequences.

Second line: Someone may increase it; the tortoise shell of ten friends cannot be opposed. Lasting correctness brings good fortune, and the king uses it to offer sacrifices to the deity, good fortune. External support brings benefit, and maintaining integrity leads to lasting good fortune.

Third line: Increase it by using unfortunate events, no blame. With sincerity, proceed in the middle way, and announce it to the duke using a jade tablet. Even adverse situations can be turned to advantage through sincerity and moderation.

Fourth line: Proceed in the middle way, announce it to the duke, and he will follow; it is beneficial to rely on this to move the state. A balanced, moderate approach wins support from higher authority for major change.

Fifth line: With sincerity and a benevolent heart, do not ask for great fortune; with sincerity, it will benefit my virtue. Acting with a pure heart naturally leads to virtue and benefit without seeking excess.
Top line: Do not increase it, or someone may strike it; if the mind is not constant, there will be misfortune. Unnecessary increase or a wavering mind provokes opposition and misfortune.

judgment_zh: 揚于王庭，孚號，有厲，告自邑，不利即戎，利有攸往。
judgment_en: Proclaimed in the royal court, there is trust in the proclamation, but there is danger. Announce it from the city; it is not beneficial to engage in warfare, but it is beneficial to have a direction to go.
lines_zh: 初九：壯于前趾，往不勝為咎。
九二：惕號，莫夜有戎，勿恤。
九三：壯于頄，有凶。君子夬夬，獨行，遇雨，若濡，有慍，无咎。
九四：臀无膚，其行次且。牽羊悔亡，聞言不信。
九五：莧陸夬夬，中行无咎。
上六：无號，終有凶。
lines_en: First line: Strength in the toes; going forward will not succeed and will bring misfortune. This warns against premature action without proper preparation.
Second line: Alarmed and calling out; at night there is danger, but do not worry. A state of alertness and caution, without excessive anxiety.

Third line: Strength in the cheeks brings misfortune. The noble person is resolute and walks alone; encountering rain and getting soaked brings annoyance, but no blame for maintaining one's integrity.

Fourth line: The buttocks have no flesh, walking is difficult; leading a sheep, regret disappears, but words are heard and not believed. Progress is hindered, though guidance removes regret; discernment is needed since words may not be trusted.
Fifth line: Resolute like a plantain, walking the middle path brings no blame. A balanced, moderate approach ensures no fault.
Top line: No proclamation; in the end, there is misfortune. Failing to take a decisive stand ultimately leads to misfortune.

judgment_en: The woman is strong; do not take this woman. The encounter involves a strong or powerful woman, advising caution against engaging with her.
lines_zh: 初六：繫于金柅，貞吉，有攸往，見凶，羸豕孚蹢躅。
九二：包有魚，无咎，不利賓。
九三：臀无膚，其行次且，厲，无大咎。
九四：包无魚，起凶。
九五：以杞包瓜，含章，有隕自天。
上九：姤其角，吝，无咎。
lines_en: First line: Tied to a metal brake; perseverance brings good fortune. If you go somewhere, you will see misfortune — a lean pig is confident but stumbles, indicating overconfidence leads to mistakes.
Second line: There is a fish in the bag; no blame, but it is not advantageous to be a guest. Something valuable is present, but one should not rely on others.
Third line: The buttocks have no flesh, movement is difficult; danger, but no great blame. A weakened state makes progress hard, though the fault is not severe.
Fourth line: The bag is empty of fish; rising brings misfortune. With nothing valuable at hand, taking action leads to misfortune.
Fifth line: Using mulberry wood to wrap a melon; containing brilliance, there is a fall from heaven. Protecting something valuable with strength, yet warning of a sudden, unexpected downfall.
Top line: Encountering its horns; regret, but no blame. Encountering something dangerous brings regret without fault.

judgment_zh: 亨。王假有廟，利見大人，亨，利貞。用大牲吉，利有攸往。
judgment_en: Success. The king approaches his temple; it is beneficial to see the great man. Success, and it is beneficial to be steadfast. Using great sacrifices brings good fortune; it is beneficial to have a goal to go towards.
lines_zh: 初六：有孚不終，乃亂乃萃，若號一握為笑，勿恤，往无咎。
六二：引吉，无咎，孚乃利用禴。
六三：萃如，嗟如，无攸利，往无咎，小吝。
九四：大吉，无咎。
九五：萃有位，无咎。匪孚，元永貞，悔亡。
上六：齎咨涕洟，无咎。
lines_en: First line: If there is sincerity but it does not last, there will be disorder and gathering; if you cry out and weep it will turn into laughter — do not worry, going forward brings no blame.
Second line: Drawing brings good fortune, no blame; sincerity is beneficial for offering a small sacrifice.
Third line: Gathering together as if sighing and lamenting; there is no benefit. Going forward brings no blame, but there is a small regret.
Fourth line: Great good fortune, no blame.
Fifth line: Gathering together in a position of authority, no blame. Even without full sincerity, great and lasting correctness will make regret disappear.
Top line: Lamenting and weeping tears and mucus, no blame.

judgment_zh: 元亨，用見大人，勿恤，南征吉。
judgment_en: Great success. It is beneficial to see a great person. Do not worry. A southward expedition brings good fortune.
lines_zh: 初六：允升，大吉。
九二：孚乃利用禴，无咎。
九三：升虛邑。
六四：王用亨于岐山，吉无咎。
六五：貞吉，升階。
上六：冥升，利于不息之貞。
lines_en: First line: True ascent, great fortune. At the beginning stage, the ascent is genuine and sincere, leading to great fortune.
Second line: Sincerity brings benefit in offering sacrifices, no blame. Sincerity in one's actions brings benefit and avoids blame.
Third line: Ascending to an empty city. Rising into a place that seems devoid of support or resources.
Fourth line: The king makes offerings on Mount Qi, good fortune, no blame.
Fifth line: Perseverance brings good fortune, ascending the steps. Steadfastness leads to good fortune, symbolized by ascending steps.
Top line: Ascending in the dark, beneficial to be steadfast and unceasing. Even in uncertain times, remaining persistent is beneficial.

judgment_zh: 亨，貞，大人吉，无咎，有言不信。
judgment_en: Success, perseverance. The great man is fortunate, no blame. Words are not trusted — in times of hardship one should remain steadfast, though communication may be mistrusted.
lines_zh: 初六：臀困于株木，入于幽谷，三歲不覿。
九二：困于酒食，朱紱方來，利用享祀，征凶，无咎。
六三：困于石，據于蒺蔾，入于其宮，不見其妻，凶。
九四：來徐徐，困于金車，吝，有終。
九五：劓刖，困于赤紱，乃徐有說，利用祭祀。
上六：困于葛藟，于臲卼，曰動悔。有悔，征吉。
lines_en: First line: Buttocks oppressed by a wooden stock, entering a secluded valley, not seen for three years. One is trapped, unable to move forward, stuck in a difficult situation for an extended period.

Second line: Oppressed by food and drink, the red sashes come; beneficial to offer sacrifices, but going forward is unlucky, no blame. Overwhelmed by indulgence, ritual is beneficial but action should be avoided for now.
Third line: Oppressed by rocks, sitting on thorns, entering his palace and not seeing his wife — misfortune. Extreme discomfort, isolation, and separation lead to misfortune.
Fourth line: Coming slowly, oppressed by a golden chariot; regret, but there is an end. A slow, burdened journey brings regret, but the hardship will eventually end.
Fifth line: Nose and feet cut off, oppressed by red sashes; then slowly there is joy, beneficial to offer sacrifices. Severe suffering under authority eventually gives way to relief and joy.

Top line: Oppressed by vines, in a precarious position, saying 'moving brings regret'; there is regret, but going forward brings good fortune. Despite entanglement and caution, taking action ultimately leads to good fortune.

judgment_zh: 改邑不改井，无喪无得，往來井井。汔至，亦未繘井，羸其瓶，凶。
judgment_en: One may change the town but not the well; there is no loss, no gain. People come and go, and the well remains the same. When it is almost reached but not yet drawn from, the jug is broken, which is unfortunate.
lines_zh: 初六：井泥不食，舊井无禽。
九二：井谷射鮒，瓮敝漏。
九三：井渫不食，為我心惻，可用汲，王明，並受其福。
六四：井甃，无咎。
九五：井冽，寒泉食。
上六：井收勿幕，有孚元吉。
lines_en: First line: The well is muddy and not fit to drink from; the old well has no birds. A neglected resource that is unusable, needing cleaning and maintenance.
Second line: In the well's valley, one shoots fish; the jug is worn out and leaks. The well is in poor condition and the tools to draw water are inadequate, suggesting a need for repair.

Third line: The well is cleaned but not yet fit to drink from, causing sorrow; it can be used to draw water, the king is enlightened and receives blessings. With further effort it can become a valuable resource.
Fourth line: The well is lined with bricks, no blame. A well that is properly constructed and maintained, signifying stability and reliability.
Fifth line: The well is clear and cold; people drink from the cold spring. A pure, beneficial resource offering nourishment.
Top line: The well is covered but not sealed; there is sincerity, and it brings great good fortune. A protected yet accessible resource, emphasizing sincerity and trust leading to great fortune.

judgment_zh: 巳日乃孚，元亨利貞，悔亡。
judgment_en: On the day trust is confirmed, there is great success; it is beneficial to be steadfast and upright, and regret disappears.
lines_zh: 初九：鞏用黃牛之革。
六二：巳日乃革之，征吉，无咎。
九三：征凶，貞厲，革言三就，有孚。
九四：悔亡，有孚改命，吉。
九五：大人虎變，未占有孚。
上六：君子豹變，小人革面，征凶，居貞吉。
lines_en: First line: Reinforce with the hide of a yellow ox — stay firm and secure before attempting any change.
Second line: When the day of completion comes, carry out the change; moving forward brings good fortune and no blame.
Third line: Acting rashly brings misfortune, and even steadfastness is risky; only after the case for change has been stated and tested three times does it win trust.
Fourth line: Regret vanishes; with sincerity one can change the mandate, bringing good fortune.
Fifth line: The great person transforms like a tiger changing its stripes — the trust is evident even before divination.

Top line: The noble person changes like a leopard changing its spots, while the petty person merely changes their face outwardly; pressing forward brings misfortune, but remaining steadfast at home brings good fortune.

judgment_en: Supreme good fortune and success.
lines_zh: 初六：鼎顛趾，利出否，得妾以其子，无咎。
九二：鼎有實，我仇有疾，不我能即，吉。
九三：鼎耳革，其行塞，雉膏不食，方雨虧悔，終吉。
九四：鼎折足，覆公餗，其形渥，凶。
六五：鼎黃耳，金鉉，利貞。
上九：鼎玉鉉，大吉，无不利。
lines_en: First line: The cauldron's legs are overturned, but this is beneficial for pouring out what has spoiled; like gaining a concubine for the sake of her son, there is no blame.
Second line: The cauldron is full; my rival is ailing and cannot come near me — good fortune.
Third line: The cauldron's handles are altered, blocking its movement, so the rich pheasant meat inside goes uneaten; but when the rain comes, regret is dissolved and the end is fortunate.
Fourth line: The cauldron's legs snap, spilling the ruler's food and staining its form — misfortune.
Fifth line: The cauldron has yellow handles and golden carrying rings; it is beneficial to remain steadfast and correct.
Top line: The cauldron has jade carrying rings — great good fortune, nothing unfavorable.

judgment_zh: 亨。震來虩虩，笑言啞啞。震驚百里，不喪匕鬯。
judgment_en: Thunder brings success. It comes with a startling rumble, followed by laughter and talk; it shocks for a hundred miles, yet does not cause the sacrificial spoon and wine to be dropped.
lines_zh: 初九：震來虩虩，後笑言啞啞，吉。
六二：震來厲，億喪貝，躋于九陵，勿逐，七日得。
六三：震蘇蘇，震行无眚。
九四：震遂泥。
六五：震往來厲，億无喪，有事。
上六：震索索，視矍矍，征凶。震不于其躬，于其鄰，无咎。婚媾有言。
lines_en: First line: Thunder comes with a startling rumble, then is followed by laughter and talk — good fortune.
Second line: Thunder comes dangerously, causing the loss of valuables; climbing to the ninth hill, do not chase after them — in seven days they will be recovered.
Third line: Thunder leaves one shaken and uneasy, but moving forward through it brings no disaster.
Fourth line: The shock of thunder sinks into the mud, becoming mired and ineffective.
Fifth line: Thunder comes and goes dangerously, but nothing of value is lost — there is still work to be done.

Top line: Thunder leaves one trembling and looking about in alarm; advancing brings misfortune. Since the thunder strikes one's neighbor and not oneself, there is no blame, though in marriage matters there will be talk.

judgment_zh: 艮其背，不獲其身，行其庭，不見其人，无咎。
judgment_en: Keeping still with the back, so that one no longer feels the body; walking in the courtyard without noticing the people there — no blame.
lines_zh: 初六：艮其趾，无咎，利永貞。
六二：艮其腓，不拯其隨，其心不快。
九三：艮其限，列其夤，厲薰心。
六四：艮其身，无咎。
六五：艮其輔，言有序，悔亡。
上九：敦艮，吉。
lines_en: First line: Keeping still at the toes — no blame; beneficial to remain perpetually correct.
Second line: Keeping still at the calves, unable to help those who would follow, leaves the heart uneasy.
Third line: Keeping still at the waist, as if splitting the spine — the resulting distress is severe and burns the heart.
Fourth line: Keeping still with the whole body — no blame.
Fifth line: Keeping still at the jaws, so that words come out orderly — regret disappears.
Top line: Genuine, wholehearted stillness — good fortune.

judgment_en: The maiden's marriage is auspicious; it is beneficial to remain steadfast and correct.
lines_zh: 初六：鴻漸于干，小子厲，有言，无咎。
六二：鴻漸于磐，飲食衎衎，吉。
九三：鴻漸于陸，夫征不復，婦孕不育，凶；利禦寇。
六四：鴻漸于木，或得其桷，无咎。
九五：鴻漸于陵，婦三歲不孕，終莫之勝，吉。
上九：鴻漸于陸，其羽可用為儀，吉.
lines_en: First line: The wild goose gradually advances to the shore; the young one is in danger and there is talk, but no blame.
Second line: The wild goose gradually advances to the boulder; eating and drinking together in harmony — good fortune.

Third line: The wild goose gradually advances to dry land; the husband goes off and does not return, the wife conceives but does not carry to term — misfortune, though it is beneficial to guard against invaders.
Fourth line: The wild goose gradually advances to a tree, perhaps finding a level branch to rest on — no blame.
Fifth line: The wild goose gradually advances to the high hill; the wife does not conceive for three years, but in the end nothing can overcome her — good fortune.
Top line: The wild goose gradually advances onto the plateau (or its flight path); its feathers can be used for ceremonial regalia — good fortune.

judgment_en: To set out brings misfortune; nothing is advantageous.
lines_zh: 初九：歸妹以娣，跛能履，征吉。
九二：眇能視，利幽人之貞。
六三：歸妹以須，反歸以娣。
九四：歸妹愆期，遲歸有時。
六五：帝乙歸妹，其君之袂，不如其娣之袂良，月幾望，吉。
上六：女承筐无實，士刲羊无血，无攸利。
lines_en: First line: The maiden marries as a younger sister/attendant; though lame, she is still able to walk — advancing is auspicious.
Second line: Though one-eyed, she is still able to see — it is beneficial to remain steadfast like a person living in quiet seclusion.
Third line: The maiden who should marry waits, then returns to marry as an attendant instead.
Fourth line: The maiden's marriage is delayed past its proper time, but a late marriage will still come in its own season.
Fifth line: Emperor Yi marries off his younger sister/daughter; the principal wife's sleeves are not as fine as those of the attending sister — as the moon nears fullness, this is auspicious.
Top line: The woman holds a basket that has nothing in it; the man stabs a sheep that yields no blood — nothing is advantageous.

judgment_en: Abundance brings success. The king attains it; do not worry — it is fitting to be like the sun at midday.
lines_zh: 初九：遇其配主，雖旬无咎，往有尚。
六二：豐其蔀，日中見斗，往得疑疾，有孚發若，吉。
九三：豐其沛，日中見沬，折其右肱，无咎。
九四：豐其蔀，日中見斗，遇其夷主，吉。
六五：來章，有慶譽，吉。
上六：豐其屋，蔀其家，闚其戶，闃其无人，三歲不覿，凶。
lines_en: First line: Meeting one's destined match — even ten days together bring no blame; going forward is honored.
Second line: The abundance is screened over, so that at midday one sees the stars of the Dipper; going forward invites suspicion and distrust, but through sincerity one is set free — good fortune.
Third line: The abundance is heavily curtained, so that at midday one sees faint stars, and the right arm is broken — no blame.
Fourth line: The abundance is screened over, so that at midday one sees the Dipper; meeting one's counterpart/equal brings good fortune.
Fifth line: Bringing forth brilliance/talented help results in praise and honor — good fortune.
Top line: The house is made abundant but the home is screened over; peering through the door, it is silent and no one is there — for three years no one is seen — misfortune.

judgment_en: Modest success; perseverance while traveling brings good fortune.
lines_zh: 初六：旅瑣瑣，斯其所取災。
六二：旅即次，懷其資，得童僕貞。
九三：旅焚其次，喪其童僕，貞厲。
九四：旅于處，得其資斧，我心不快。
六五：射雉一矢亡，終以譽命。
上九：鳥焚其巢，旅人先笑後號咷。喪牛于易，凶。
lines_en: First line: The wanderer is petty and preoccupied with trivial matters, and this is what invites disaster.
Second line: The wanderer arrives at a lodging place, keeps hold of his resources, and gains a loyal young attendant.
Third line: The wanderer's lodging burns down and he loses his young attendant — even steadfastness here is perilous.
Fourth line: The wanderer finds a resting place and gains resources and tools, yet his heart is not at ease.
Fifth line: In shooting a pheasant he loses a single arrow, but in the end this brings him praise and a mandate/position.
Top line: The bird's nest burns; the wanderer laughs at first, then wails and cries — losing an ox at Yi with ease brings misfortune.

judgment_en: Minor success. It is beneficial to have somewhere to go. It is beneficial to see a great person.
lines_zh: 初六：進退，利武人之貞。
九二：巽在床下，用史巫紛若，吉，无咎。
九三：頻巽，吝。
六四：悔亡，田獲三品。
九五：貞吉悔亡，无不利。无初有終，先庚三日，後庚三日，吉。
上九：巽在床下，喪其資斧，貞凶。
lines_en: First line: Advancing and retreating; it is beneficial for a warrior to be steadfast.
Second line: Xùn is under the bed. Using historians and shamans, there is confusion, but it is auspicious and without blame.
Third line: Repeatedly yielding, there is distress.
Fourth line: Regret vanishes. In the field, three kinds of game are caught.

Fifth line: Steadfast and upright, good fortune, regret vanishes. There is nothing that is not beneficial. No beginning, but there is an end. Three days before Geng, three days after Geng, auspicious.
Top line: Xùn is under the bed. Losing one's tools and resources, steadfastness leads to misfortune.

judgment_en: Success. It is beneficial to be steadfast and upright.
lines_zh: 初九：和兌，吉。
九二：孚兌，吉，悔亡。
六三：來兌，凶。
九四：商兌，未寧，介疾有喜。
九五：孚于剝，有厲。
上六：引兌。
lines_en: First line: Harmonious joy, good fortune. When joy is harmonious and balanced, it brings good fortune.
Second line: Sincere joy, good fortune, regret vanishes. Sincere and genuine joy leads to good fortune and the disappearance of regrets.
Third line: Coming joy, misfortune. Seeking joy from external sources or in an inappropriate manner can lead to misfortune.
Fourth line: Trading joy, not yet at peace, there is joy in overcoming difficulties.
Fifth line: Sincerity towards stripping away, there is danger. Being sincere in removing superficial layers or falsehoods can be dangerous.
Top line: Leading joy — guiding joy implies a higher level of understanding or mastery over joy.

judgment_zh: 亨。王假有廟，利涉大川，利貞。
judgment_en: Success. The king approaches his temple. It is beneficial to cross the great river. It is beneficial to be steadfast and correct.
lines_zh: 初六：用拯馬壯，吉。
九二：渙奔其机，悔亡。
六三：渙其躬，无悔。
六四：渙其群，元吉。渙有丘，匪夷所思。
九五：渙汗其大號，渙王居，无咎。
上九：渙其血，去逖出，无咎。
lines_en: First line: Use a strong horse to rescue. Good fortune. This suggests taking decisive and strong action to overcome difficulties.
Second line: Dispersion leads to the running of the machine, regret vanishes. When dispersion occurs, things start moving and functioning again, resolving issues.
Third line: Dispersion of the self, no regret. This advises letting go of personal ego and self-centeredness.
Fourth line: Dispersion of the group, great fortune. Dispersion leads to a mound, beyond ordinary thoughts — breaking up a collective can lead to higher wisdom or success.
Fifth line: Dispersion sweats its great command, the king's residence is dispersed, no blame. Even the highest authorities must sometimes undergo dissolution for renewal.
Top line: Dispersion of blood, go far away and emerge, no blame. A complete and thorough transformation, necessary though drastic.

judgment_en: Success. Bitter restraint, not permissible to be steadfast.
lines_zh: 初九：不出戶庭，无咎。
九二：不出門庭，凶。
六三：不節若，則嗟若，无咎。
六四：安節，亨。
九五：甘節，吉；往有尚。
上六：苦節，貞凶，悔亡。
lines_en: First line: Do not leave the courtyard, no blame. Staying within one's own boundaries and not venturing out leads to no harm.
Second line: Do not leave the gate, misfortune. Staying too confined and not engaging with the outside world can lead to misfortune; balance is necessary.
Third line: If one is not restrained, then there will be lamentation, no blame. The consequence is a natural result of one's actions, not a fault.
Fourth line: Peaceful restraint, success. A calm and balanced approach to moderation leads to success.
Fifth line: Sweet restraint, good fortune; there is honor in going forward. Practicing restraint pleasantly brings good fortune.
Top line: Bitter restraint, steadfastness leads to misfortune, regret disappears. Severe restraint leads to misfortune if persisted in, though the regret eventually fades.

judgment_en: Pigs and fishes are auspicious. It is favorable to cross the great river. It is favorable to be steadfast and upright.
lines_zh: 初九：虞吉，有它不燕。
九二：鳴鶴在陰，其子和之，我有好爵，吾與爾靡之。
六三：得敵，或鼓或罷，或泣或歌。
六四：月幾望，馬匹亡，无咎。
九五：有孚攣如，无咎。
上九：翰音登于天，貞凶。
lines_en: First line: Auspicious for preparation; there is something else, not at ease. Initial efforts are favorable, but there may be some unease or distraction.
Second line: A calling crane in the shade, its young respond harmoniously; I have a good goblet, I will share it with you. Harmonious communication and sharing lead to mutual benefit.
Third line: Encountering an adversary: sometimes beating the drum, sometimes stopping; sometimes weeping, sometimes singing. Facing opposition, emotions fluctuate between extremes.
Fourth line: The moon is nearly full, the horse is lost, no blame. Nearing fulfillment, some losses may occur, but there is no fault.
Fifth line: There is sincerity, like being bound together, no blame. Sincerity and unity bring about a situation free of fault.
Top line: The sound of wings ascends to heaven, steadfastness leads to misfortune. Aspirations that reach too high may lead to misfortune despite good intentions.

judgment_zh: 亨，利貞，可小事，不可大事。飛鳥遺之音，不宜上，宜下，大吉。
judgment_en: Success. It is beneficial to be correct. Small matters are feasible, but not large matters. The sound of a flying bird is left behind. It is not suitable to go upward, but suitable to go downward. Great fortune.
lines_zh: 初六：飛鳥以凶。
六二：過其祖，遇其妣；不及其君，遇其臣；无咎。
九三：弗過防之，從或戕之，凶。
九四：无咎，弗過遇之。往厲必戒，勿用永貞。
六五：密雲不雨，自我西郊，公弋取彼在穴。
上六：弗遇過之，飛鳥離之，凶，是謂災眚。
lines_en: First line: A flying bird brings misfortune. Attempting to rise too high or act prematurely at the beginning can lead to misfortune.
Second line: Surpassing one's grandfather, encountering one's grandmother; not reaching one's lord, encountering one's minister; no blame. One should not aim too high but find a middle ground.
Third line: If one does not surpass and guards against it, following this may lead to injury, misfortune. This warns against excessive caution or over-guarding.
Fourth line: No blame, not surpassing and encountering it. Going forward with caution is necessary; do not use eternal correctness — adapt rather than being overly rigid.
Fifth line: Dense clouds but no rain, coming from our western outskirts. The duke shoots and catches it in the cave. This symbolizes potential not yet realized, and taking initiative to realize it.
Top line: Not encountering and surpassing it, the flying bird leaves it, misfortune. This is called disaster and calamity — ignoring or surpassing problems can lead to disaster.

judgment_en: Success. It is beneficial to be steadfast and correct in small matters. In the beginning, there is good fortune, but in the end, there is disorder.
lines_zh: 初九：曳其輪，濡其尾，无咎。
六二：婦喪其茀，勿逐，七日得。
九三：高宗伐鬼方，三年克之，小人勿用。
六四：繻有衣袽，終日戒。
九五：東鄰殺牛，不如西鄰之禴祭，實受其福。
上六：濡其首，厲。
lines_en: First line: Dragging its wheels, wetting its tail, no blame. At the beginning there is some difficulty, but there is no blame for this initial struggle.
Second line: The woman loses her veil; do not pursue; in seven days it will be obtained. A temporary loss that will be recovered after a period if not chased.
Third line: King Gaozong attacks the Gui Fang; after three years he conquers them; do not employ petty people. This suggests perseverance and warns against employing unworthy people.
Fourth line: There are clothes with patches; be cautious all day long. Advises vigilance even when things seem patched up or imperfect.

Fifth line: The eastern neighbor slaughters a cow; it is not as good as the western neighbor's simple sacrifice; truly receive its blessing. Sincerity and simplicity are more effective than grand but insincere actions.
Top line: Wetting its head; danger. At the end, there is a warning of danger — even after completion, one must remain cautious to avoid peril.

judgment_zh: 亨，小狐汔濟，濡其尾，无攸利。
judgment_en: Success. The little fox, almost across, gets its tail wet. Nothing is favorable.
lines_zh: 初六：濡其尾，吝。
九二：曳其輪，貞吉。
六三：未濟，征凶，利涉大川。
九四：貞吉，悔亡，震用伐鬼方，三年有賞于大國。
六五：貞吉，无悔，君子之光，有孚，吉。
上九：有孚于飲酒，无咎，濡其首，有孚失是。
lines_en: First line: Getting its tail wet, there is humiliation. Warns of the dangers of premature action; minor but avoidable mistakes can lead to embarrassment.
Second line: Dragging its wheels, perseverance brings good fortune. Careful, slow progress leads to good fortune.

Third line: Before Completion, going forward brings misfortune. It is favorable to cross the great river. Moving forward without preparation can lead to misfortune, but significant challenges can be beneficial if handled correctly.

Fourth line: Perseverance brings good fortune, regret vanishes. Thunder is used to attack the demon's realm; in three years there will be rewards in the great country. Bold, well-timed action leads to significant rewards.
Fifth line: Perseverance brings good fortune, no regret. The light of the noble person, there is trust, good fortune.
Top line: There is trust in drinking wine, no blame. Getting its head wet, there is trust but it is lost. Vigilance is necessary even in seemingly secure situations.
